philosophy: Data fluency is interpretation, not computation. No concept in this taxonomy requires calculation beyond mean, median, and proportion. The goal is shared vocabulary and reading skill across Harvard staff.

foundational.description: What data is and how it gets shaped

communication.description: How data gets used and misused

concepts.title: What shape your data takes
concepts.outcome: Learner can describe the shape of a distribution (symmetric, skewed, bimodal) and explain why shape matters before any single number is reported.

concepts.title: What 'typical' really means
concepts.outcome: Learner can identify when mean and median differ substantially and explain which is more honest in skewed contexts.

concepts.title: Why the spread matters as much as the center
concepts.outcome: Learner recognizes that two groups with the same average can differ wildly in spread, and that spread changes what conclusions are defensible.

concepts.title: Who is actually in the data
concepts.outcome: Learner asks who is included before interpreting any aggregate, and recognizes when a sample cannot speak for the population.

concepts.title: What was actually measured
concepts.outcome: Learner distinguishes the construct (what we mean) from the measure (what we capture), and identifies the gap.

concepts.title: Snapshot or follow-along
concepts.outcome: Learner identifies whether a dataset tracks a group over time (cohort) or a single moment (cross-section), and what each can and cannot say.

concepts.title: Talking about everyone vs talking about some
concepts.outcome: Learner uses inferential language carefully, distinguishing claims about a sample from claims about a population.

concepts.title: When numbers are not numbers
concepts.outcome: Learner identifies when a number is a label (zip code, ID, ranking) versus a measurable quantity, and treats each appropriately.

concepts.title: When one value dominates
concepts.outcome: Learner recognizes outliers and asks whether to keep, exclude, or report them separately, based on context.

concepts.title: When the distribution leans
concepts.outcome: Learner identifies right- and left-skewed distributions and predicts which summary statistic will mislead.

concepts.title: What's the denominator
concepts.outcome: Learner asks 'compared to what?' before reading any rate or proportion, and recognizes when a baseline is missing.

concepts.title: Three different things people call the same word
concepts.outcome: Learner distinguishes rates, ratios, and proportions and recognizes when one is being used in place of another.

concepts.title: A 5 percent rise is not 5 percentage points
concepts.outcome: Learner separates percentage change from percentage-point change and reads each correctly.

concepts.title: The third variable in the room
concepts.outcome: Learner identifies plausible confounders before accepting a relationship between two variables.

concepts.title: Who got into the data and who did not
concepts.outcome: Learner identifies systematic patterns in who is included or excluded and how this distorts conclusions.

concepts.title: Why the gap is part of the story
concepts.outcome: Learner asks why values are missing and recognizes that missingness is rarely random.

concepts.title: Averages of averages can mislead
concepts.outcome: Learner identifies when data has been aggregated and asks what the disaggregation might reveal.

concepts.title: Trends, noise, and pretending you can tell them apart
concepts.outcome: Learner distinguishes signal from noise in time-ordered data and resists narratives built on two adjacent points.

concepts.title: The most famous pitfall
concepts.outcome: Learner produces alternative explanations for a reported correlation and identifies what evidence would support causal claims.

concepts.title: Axes lie, choices lie, defaults lie
concepts.outcome: Learner reads chart axes, scales, and aggregation choices critically before accepting the visual claim.

concepts.title: Rows, columns, and what is being compared
concepts.outcome: Learner identifies the comparison being made in a table and recognizes how row vs column percentages tell different stories.

concepts.title: A big-enough number is not necessarily a meaningful number
concepts.outcome: Learner separates 'statistically detectable' from 'big enough to matter' and asks which one is being claimed.

concepts.title: What an error bar is actually telling you
concepts.outcome: Learner reads confidence intervals as a measure of estimation precision, not certainty about a value.

concepts.title: When you can fairly say two groups differ
concepts.outcome: Learner identifies the conditions under which a group comparison is fair and the conditions under which it is misleading.

concepts.title: Saying you do not know without losing trust
concepts.outcome: Learner communicates uncertainty in their own reports without hedging into uselessness or pretending to false precision.
